CN-01HIGHBrowse & Search
No Compatibility Filter in Primary Navigation or Category Tiles
The desktop hero has a vehicle-selector widget (Make/Model/Year/Trim) but it doesn't connect to the category tile CTAs below. A visitor who picks `2018 Subaru WRX` and clicks `Shop Performance` lands on a generic Performance collection with no fitment filter applied and no `Showing parts for your WRX` confirmation. Baymard's research documents 65% task failure when compatibility filters are absent or unclear — for a fitment-dependent catalog, this is the single biggest conversion gap on the homepage.
What changes: Wire the selector to pass vehicle parameters to category tile CTA URLs. Display a persistent `Showing parts for: [Year] [Make] [Model]` chip on every collection page when a vehicle has been selected.

Element

`(absent — vehicle selector exists in hero but does not propagate to category tile CTAs)`

Observation

AWDMods sells compatibility-dependent products. The homepage hero includes a vehicle selector (Make / Model / Year / Trim), but it is visually buried in the hero image and disconnected from the five category tiles below. Once a visitor selects their vehicle and clicks `Shop Performance`, there is no visible confirmation that search results will be filtered to their fitment. Baymard's compatibility filter research shows 65% of users fail to find the correct product when compatibility filtering is absent or unclear.

Recommendation

Connect the homepage vehicle selector to the category tile CTAs so that clicking `Shop Performance` (or any tile) passes the selected vehicle as a URL parameter and renders the target collection page pre-filtered to that fitment. Display a persistent `Showing parts for: [Year] [Make] [Model]` chip at the top of every collection page when a vehicle has been selected. If the selector is not yet wired to filtering, add a prominent `Select your vehicle` prompt above the category tiles.

Why this matters

When compatibility filtering is absent or invisible, customers click through to collection pages, scroll past incompatible parts, and abandon — often buying from a competitor whose site surfaces fitment-specific results immediately. For a performance parts store serving Focus RS, WRX, and STI owners, fitment confidence is the highest-leverage conversion lever at the category navigation level.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e1
  • DOM · e17
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    The header shows a wide dark input box labeled 'Search' that visually appears as a text input, but baton element e1 is role=button (44x44px at x:1246, y:56) — a search icon toggle, not the expanded input field. The expanded input (e17, role=combobox, 929px wide) sits at the same y-position but is only present when the icon is activated.

Source

Search And Filter UX
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
CN-02HIGHBrowse & Search
No Compatibility Filter in Primary Navigation or Category Tiles
On mobile, the vehicle compatibility finder lives inside the hero section but doesn't follow visitors into category pages — once a shopper selects their vehicle and lands on a collection, there's no `Showing parts for: Ford Focus RS` chip and no way to re-filter by vehicle without going back. The hamburger's `Shop by Vehicle` requires re-entering the vehicle from scratch each session.
What changes: Persist the vehicle selection in localStorage after FIND PARTS submission. Surface a sticky `Vehicle: Ford Focus RS` chip below the site header that follows visitors into category pages and can be changed or cleared.

Element

(absent)

Observation

AWDMods sells compatibility-dependent products — a cold-air intake for a Ford Focus RS will not fit a Subaru WRX STI. The homepage hero includes a vehicle selector (Select Make / Model / Year / Trim), but it is visually buried in the hero image and disconnected from the five category tiles below. Once a visitor selects their vehicle and clicks 'Shop Performance,' there is no visible confirmation that search results will be filtered to their fitment. Baymard's compatibility filter research shows that 65% of users fail to find the correct product when compatibility filtering is absent or unclear, and 32% of sites selling fitment-dependent products lack this filter entirely.

Recommendation

Connect the homepage vehicle selector to the category tile CTAs so that clicking 'Shop Performance' (or any tile) passes the selected vehicle as a URL parameter and renders the target collection page pre-filtered to that fitment. Display a persistent 'Showing parts for: [Year] [Make] [Model]' banner at the top of every collection page when a vehicle has been selected. If the selector is not yet wired to filtering, add a prominent 'Select your vehicle' prompt above the category tiles with a clear affordance that it will refine results.

Why this matters

When compatibility filtering is absent or invisible, customers click through to collection pages, scroll past incompatible parts, and abandon — often buying from a competitor whose site surfaces fitment-specific results immediately. For a performance parts store serving Ford Focus RS, WRX, and STI owners, fitment confidence is the single highest-leverage conversion lever at the category navigation level.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    The hero contains a vehicle selector widget (Select Make / Select Model / Select Year / Select your Trim) but it is visually embedded in the hero banner background image and not connected to the category navigation tiles below it. The category tiles (Performance, Handling & Brakes, Interior, Exterior, Electronics) link to broad subcategory collections with no indication that results will be filtered to the visitor's specific vehicle.
  • Visual · section-2.jpg @ y=513 (desktop)
    Category tiles at section-2 show subcategory text (e.g., 'Intakes Exhaust Cooling Drivetrain upgrades') but carry no fitment-filter context. No 'filtered to your vehicle' indicator appears on or beneath any tile.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
CN-03MEDIUMBrowse & Search
Breadcrumbs Absent on Homepage — No Hierarchy or History Type Present
There are no breadcrumbs anywhere on the site — neither hierarchy breadcrumbs (`Home > Performance`) nor history breadcrumbs (`← Back to results`). Two SearchPilot controlled tests found that removing breadcrumbs cost -5.5% and -7% organic traffic respectively. The schema JSON-LD also has no BreadcrumbList markup. For an SEO-dependent store competing on automotive parts keywords, this is structurally avoidable traffic loss.
What changes: Implement BreadcrumbList JSON-LD across all collection and product pages. For collection pages, render `Home > [Category]` with crawlable anchor links. For PDPs, add both a hierarchy breadcrumb and a `Back to [Collection]` history link.

Element

`(absent — no BreadcrumbList in JSON-LD; no breadcrumb component below nav)`

Observation

No breadcrumb navigation is present on the AWDMods homepage in any form — neither a hierarchy type (`Home > Performance`) nor a history type (`← Back to results`). The page's schema JSON-LD contains OnlineStore and WebSite types but no BreadcrumbList markup. While breadcrumbs are typically most critical on PDPs and collection pages, their absence from the Shopify theme means they are likely missing across the site. Two SearchPilot controlled tests found removing breadcrumbs caused -5.5% and -7% organic traffic loss respectively.

Recommendation

Implement BreadcrumbList JSON-LD schema across all collection and product pages. For collection pages, the hierarchy breadcrumb should render as `Home > [Category]` with crawlable anchor links. For PDPs, add both a hierarchy breadcrumb (`Home > Performance > Intakes > [Product Name]`) and a history-type `Back to [Collection] ([N] items)` link that preserves the visitor's filter and sort state.

Why this matters

Breadcrumb absence costs organic traffic directly (-5.5% to -7% in two controlled tests) and degrades navigation confidence for users stepping back up the category hierarchy. For an SEO-dependent store competing on automotive parts keywords, this is a structurally avoidable traffic loss.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e12
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=275 (desktop)
    The five category tiles (Performance, Handling & Brakes, Interior, Exterior, Electronics) each display a subcategory list and a 'Shop [Category]' CTA button. None display a product count — e.g., 'Performance (127 parts)'. The tile text lists subcategory names only.

Source

Breadcrumbs

CN-04MEDIUMBrowse & Search
Breadcrumbs Absent on Homepage — No Hierarchy or History Type Present
The mobile sticky header has a full-width search bar visible on every page load — no icon-tap required. This is the textbook pattern for mobile persistent search: high-intent visitors who arrive with a specific query can search immediately without hunting for an entry point.
What changes: No change needed. Verify the search input remains visible at scroll_y=0 across the 390px viewport range.

Element

(absent)

Observation

No breadcrumb navigation is present on the AWDMods homepage in any form — neither a hierarchy type ('Home > Performance') nor a history type ('← Back to results'). The page's schema JSON-LD (baton page_head.schema_jsonld) contains OnlineStore and WebSite types but no BreadcrumbList markup. While breadcrumbs are typically most critical on PDPs and collection pages, their absence from the Shopify theme architecture means they are likely missing across the site — confirmed absent in the schema data. Two independent SearchPilot controlled tests found that removing breadcrumbs caused -5.5% and -7% organic traffic loss respectively.

Recommendation

Implement BreadcrumbList JSON-LD schema across all collection and product pages. For collection pages, the hierarchy breadcrumb should render as 'Home > [Category]' with crawlable anchor links. For PDPs, add both a hierarchy breadcrumb ('Home > Performance > Intakes > [Product Name]') and a history-type 'Back to [Collection] ([N] items)' link that preserves the visitor's filter and sort state from their previous collection page.

Why this matters

Breadcrumb absence costs organic traffic directly (two controlled tests, -5.5% to -7%) and degrades navigation confidence for users who want to step back up the category hierarchy after landing on a product page. For an SEO-dependent store competing on automotive parts keywords, this is a structurally avoidable traffic loss.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    The header contains logo, search, 'Select Your Vehicle', account, and cart elements. No breadcrumb trail is visible beneath the main navigation bar. The nav bar (e18) contains 'Shop All' and 'Shop by Category' but no breadcrumb component.
  • Visual · section-3.jpg @ y=876 (desktop)
    Featured Collection product cards render with brand name, product title, price, and badge but no breadcrumb or back-to-collection link on the homepage product display. The page_head.schema_jsonld contains no BreadcrumbList schema type.

CN-05LOWBrowse & Search
Vehicle Selector Widget Present Above the Fold on Homepage
AWDMods correctly surfaces a Make/Model/Year/Trim vehicle selector in the desktop hero, visible above the fold. This is the right pattern for an automotive parts store — fitment-first navigation positioned where visitors can self-select before browsing the catalog.
What changes: Placement is correct. The remaining gap is wiring the submit button to pass vehicle parameters into category tile URLs — see `category-navigation F-02`.

Element

`button` at e3 (x=795, y=116, content `Shop by Category`)

Observation

AWDMods correctly surfaces a four-field vehicle selector (Make / Model / Year / Trim) in the hero banner, visible above the fold at page load. This addresses the core compatibility navigation need for automotive parts shoppers and positions fitment-first navigation prominently.

Recommendation

The vehicle selector placement is correct. Wire the submit button to pass vehicle parameters to collection page URLs so filter state persists across navigation. See `category-navigation F-02` for the gap in how selector results connect to category tile CTAs.

Why this matters

Automotive parts stores that surface vehicle compatibility filtering above the fold see higher engagement from returning customers who bookmark their vehicle configuration.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e10
  • DOM · e11
  • Visual · section-2.jpg @ y=513 (desktop)
    The Featured Collection row in section-2 shows 5 product cards per row at 1920px viewport width. Baton confirms the first two cards (e10 at x:240, e11 at x:528) have 271px widths with ~17px gutters — consistent with a 5-column grid across the 1905px page width. Jenkins (2020) measured a 35.7% increase in per-row scanning time at 5 columns vs. 4 columns.

CF-01HIGHCheckout Experience
No Express Checkout Buttons on Homepage Buy Path
Payment method logos on mobile only appear in the footer at scroll_y ~1629 — visible only after a visitor scrolls past the newsletter section. Nothing near the product cards or in the cart drawer reassures shoppers that PayPal or Shop Pay is available before they commit to checkout.
What changes: Surface familiar payment logos (especially PayPal and Shop Pay) near the product-card buy buttons or inside the cart drawer summary. Static display is sufficient — the icons don't need to be interactive.

Element

(absent)

Observation

Apple Pay, Google Pay, and Shop Pay buttons are entirely absent from the homepage buy path. The footer lists these payment methods as small brand icons at scroll position y=2841, but they are static logo images with no checkout action. There are no express checkout buttons in the cart icon area, no cart-drawer with an express option, and no quick-add-to-cart flow on the featured product cards. Visitors who want to use a digital wallet have no accelerated path from the homepage.

Recommendation

If this store runs on Shopify (which the page structure confirms — Shopify CDN URLs, Shopify checkout redirect), enable Shop Pay, Apple Pay, and Google Pay through Shopify Payments and display the express checkout buttons in the cart drawer. When a visitor clicks the cart icon, the cart drawer should show Shop Pay, Apple Pay, and Google Pay buttons above the 'Checkout' button. For stores with a checkout page at /checkout, position wallet buttons at the top of the checkout flow above the email/login field, per Stripe's controlled A/B data showing ~2x conversion at top-of-flow versus end-of-flow placement.

Why this matters

Apple Pay alone delivers +22.3% conversion lift (Stripe controlled A/B). Shop Pay converts approximately 9% higher across all checkouts. For a specialty performance parts store with AOV in the $135–$1,766 range, the incremental revenue from a 9–22% conversion lift on wallet-enabled visitors is material. Every visitor on macOS Safari or iOS who has Apple Pay configured is being offered a slower path than their device supports.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    Header area shows a cart icon (top right) with no cart-drawer, no mini-cart, and no Apple Pay / Google Pay / Shop Pay express checkout buttons anywhere in the visible header or hero.
  • Visual · section-5.jpg @ y=2073 (desktop)
    Footer payment icons row shows Amex, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard, PayPal, Shop Pay, and Visa logos — but these are static brand-recognition icons, not actionable express checkout buttons.

Source

Research reference
SILVER
CF-02LOWCheckout Experience
Free Shipping Threshold Visible Above Fold
The free-shipping threshold is announced at the very top of the desktop page, before any scroll — the earliest possible disclosure point. Shoppers see it before engaging with any product, removing the late-funnel surprise that drives most cart abandonment.
What changes: Keep the placement. Add a cart-level progress indicator (`Add $X more for free shipping`) once items are in cart. Tighten `on most orders` to either `on orders $75+` (if everything qualifies) or a specific exclusion link.

Element

`banner` at e19 (y=0, content `FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+ — Contiguous US only`)

Observation

The free-shipping threshold ($75+) is disclosed in a persistent announcement bar at the very top of the page, above the header, at scroll position y=0. Shoppers see this before engaging with any product — the earliest possible disclosure point in the funnel.

Recommendation

The threshold is correctly placed. Add a cart-level threshold progress indicator (`Add $X more for free shipping`) to the cart drawer once items are present. Tighten the copy: replace `on most orders` with either `on orders $75+` (if all standard-size orders qualify) or a specific exclusion link.

Why this matters

Shipping cost revealed late is the single largest actionable abandonment driver (39–48% of abandoning shoppers, Baymard). Early disclosure at the top of the homepage prevents this trigger from firing at checkout.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e37
  • Visual · section-5.jpg @ y=2073 (desktop)
    Footer payment icons row (y~2841) shows Amex, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard, PayPal, Shop Pay, and Visa. No Afterpay, Klarna, or Affirm logo is present among the 8 payment icons.

Source

Checkout Optimization
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
CF-03LOWCheckout Experience
Payment Method Breadth Meets Minimum Threshold
AWDMods accepts 8 payment methods (American Express, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard, PayPal, Shop Pay, Visa) — well above the 4-method minimum that prevents payment-driven abandonment. The breadth is good.
What changes: No change needed on breadth. The actionable gap is making these methods active checkout buttons in the cart drawer rather than static footer logos — see `checkout-flows F-02`.

Element

`list` at e37 (y=2841)

Observation

Eight payment methods are shown in the footer: American Express, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard, PayPal, Shop Pay, Visa. This exceeds the 4-method minimum (cards, PayPal, Apple Pay, Google Pay) that Baymard identifies as the floor for avoiding payment-method-driven abandonment.

Recommendation

The payment method breadth is sufficient. The actionable gap is that these methods need to be surfaced as live express checkout buttons in the cart drawer and checkout flow (see `checkout-flows F-02`), not only as static brand logos in the footer.

Why this matters

Meeting the minimum payment method threshold removes the 10% payment-method abandonment trigger. Surfacing these methods actively at the cart converts the passive footer assurance into an active conversion lift.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e2
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    Top-right header area shows a cart icon (bag icon) with no item count badge visible and no subtotal displayed. The icon is a plain anchor link to /cart with no drawer functionality triggered on hover or click visible in the DOM.

Source

Checkout Optimization
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
CS-01HIGHSearch Findability
Title Tag Is 18 Characters and Duplicates Brand Name
The desktop page title is `AWDMods - AWDMods` — only 18 characters, just the brand name twice. Google rewrites titles under 30 characters more than 95% of the time. The title gives Google no signal about Ford Focus RS, WRX, performance parts, or anything someone would actually search for — so Google substitutes its own version, often built from random nav text.
What changes: Rewrite the title to a 51–60 character formula that names the products and vehicles: `Performance Parts for Ford Focus RS, ST & WRX | AWDMods`.

Element

`<title>` (content `AWDMods - AWDMods`, 18 chars)

Observation

The HTML title tag reads `AWDMods - AWDMods` — 18 characters total, with the brand name repeated on both sides of the separator. Zyppy's Q1 2025 analysis of 81,000 titles found that titles under 30 characters are rewritten by Google more than 95% of the time. The title contains zero product-category or keyword content: no mention of Ford Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX/STI, performance parts, or automotive accessories. For a shopper searching `Ford Focus RS performance parts` or `WRX STI exhaust`, this title provides no signal that AWDMods is relevant.

Recommendation

Rewrite the homepage title to the formula `[Primary Category] - [Key Differentiator] | [Brand]`, targeting 51–60 characters. Example: `Performance Parts for Ford Focus RS, ST & WRX | AWDMods` (54 chars). Front-load the product category since AWDMods is not yet brand-dominant for its target queries. Edit `layout/theme.liquid` or the SEO snippet that emits the title tag; ensure the home template overrides any default Shopify title pattern.

Why this matters

A title rewritten by Google to something generic eliminates AWDMods' control over its first-impression messaging in search results. Every transactional query where the title fails to signal relevance is a lost click to better-titled competitors.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · baton.page_head.title
    Title reads "AWDMods - AWDMods" — 18 characters, brand name repeated twice, no product category or keyword content.

Source

Title Formulas SERP Psychology
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
CS-02HIGHSearch Findability
Meta Description Is Absent — Homepage Has No SERP Snippet Copy
The mobile page has no meta description, and the open-graph description (used for social previews) is just `AWDMods` — the brand name and nothing else. Google generates its own snippet from random page text, and every social link preview shows `AWDMods` as the description.
What changes: Write a 140–155 character meta description — `Shop performance parts, exterior mods, and accessories for the Ford Focus RS, Focus ST, Subaru WRX, and STI. Free shipping on orders $75+.` Apply the same text to og:description.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The homepage has no meta description tag. When a meta description is absent, Google auto-generates snippet text by extracting sentences from the page body — typically resulting in navigation labels, footer link text, or newsletter copy rather than a persuasive value proposition. For awdmods.com, Google is likely generating something from the category tile labels ("Performance Intakes Exhaust Cooling Drivetrain upgrades Shop Performance") or the newsletter paragraph, neither of which communicates the store's value proposition to a search user.

Recommendation

Add a meta description between 140–155 characters that leads with the primary customer benefit and names the key vehicle fitments: for example, "Shop performance parts, exhaust systems, and exterior upgrades for the Ford Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, and STI. Free shipping on most orders over $75." This gives Google a quality candidate description and directly communicates shipping value to the shopper before they click.

Why this matters

The homepage meta description controls first-impression copy for all branded and category queries where the site ranks. Auto-generated snippets drawn from navigation or footer text consistently underperform hand-written descriptions — this is uncontrolled messaging on the most-crawled page of the site.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · baton.page_head.meta_description
    baton.page_head.meta_description is null — no meta description tag present in server-rendered HTML.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
CS-03HIGHSearch Findability
Homepage JSON-LD Lacks Product, Offer, AggregateRating, and GTIN Schema
The mobile homepage has structured data for store identity and site search, but no Product, Offer, AggregateRating, or return-policy schema. Featured product cards display prices and stars on the page — but Google can't see any of it as structured data, blocking eligibility for Shopping rich results, ChatGPT Shopping, and star-rating SERP snippets.
What changes: Add Product JSON-LD blocks for each featured product including `name`, `image`, `offers`, and `aggregateRating` where reviews exist. Update the homepage Liquid template to inject the schema for each product.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The page's structured data consists of two schema blocks: @type OnlineStore (with name, logo, sameAs social links, and url) and @type WebSite (with SearchAction for site search). Neither block contains Product, Offer, AggregateRating, MerchantReturnPolicy, or any product identifier (GTIN, MPN, SKU). The featured collection carousel (sections featured-collection-top and featured-collection-bottom, visible in section-3.jpg and section-4.jpg) displays ten products with prices and two products with visible star ratings — element e21 shows "5.0 / 5.0 (2) 2 total reviews" and element e22 shows star imagery. This review data exists in the page DOM but is invisible to Google's structured data parser. SearchPilot's controlled split test found that adding AggregateRating schema alone produces approximately 20% organic traffic uplift. That uplift is unreachable while no Product or AggregateRating schema exists. Additionally, without GTIN fields in schema, AWDMods products cannot be matched in Google Shopping's knowledge graph or in ChatGPT Instant Checkout (launched September 29, 2025).

Recommendation

For the homepage featured collection cards: implement JSON-LD Product schema blocks for each featured product, including at minimum: name, url, offers (price, priceCurrency, availability), aggregateRating (ratingValue, ratingCount) for products with reviews, and gtin or mpn for each product. For products like the VelourTex floor mats (e10) and Revo Designs decal kit (e11) that carry accessible names with full product titles, those names are the correct schema name values. If the homepage schema cannot list all featured products, prioritize adding it at the product-page (PDP) level first — that is where the deeper structured data impact lands. At the store level, add MerchantReturnPolicy to the OnlineStore block using Option B (merchantReturnLink pointing to the existing return policy page).

Why this matters

Missing AggregateRating schema is a direct forfeit of the star-rating display that drives 20% organic traffic uplift in controlled testing. Missing GTIN data blocks AWDMods products from appearing in Google Shopping rich results and ChatGPT Instant Checkout product matching — both channels that route high-intent buyers who have already pre-qualified their intent before clicking.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · baton.page_head.schema_jsonld
    schema_jsonld contains two blocks: @type OnlineStore and @type WebSite. No Product, Offer, AggregateRating, MerchantReturnPolicy, or GTIN fields present anywhere on the page.
  • Visual · section-3.jpg @ y=876 (desktop)
    Featured collection shows product cards with prices (FROM $135.99, $405.48, FROM $1,649.99, $19.99) and star ratings (5.0/5.0 with 2 reviews, 5.0/5.0 with 3 reviews) — review data and pricing exist on the page but are absent from structured data.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
CS-04MEDIUMSearch Findability
No Open Graph Image — Social and AI Thumbnail Sharing Is Unbranded
The mobile homepage has no descriptive body copy — no store description, no positioning statement, nothing that answers `What does AWDMods sell?` and `Why this store?` AI shopping agents (ChatGPT Shopping, Perplexity) that crawl the page for those answers find nothing extractable, so AWDMods doesn't surface in their recommendations.
What changes: Add a 2–3 sentence store description between the hero vehicle finder and the category carousel — `AWDMods specializes in performance, exterior, and interior upgrades for the Ford Focus RS, Focus ST, Subaru WRX, and WRX STI. Every part is fitment-tested for your platform.`

Element

(absent)

Observation

The page has no og:image meta tag. When the homepage URL is shared on social platforms (Facebook, LinkedIn, iMessage link previews) or surfaced by AI shopping agents that use Open Graph data for thumbnail rendering, no image is provided. The platform either shows a blank thumbnail or attempts to scrape the first available image on the page, which is unpredictable. The site has a high-quality hero image of a Subaru WRX STI in a cinematic low-light setting (visible at scroll_y 0) that would make an effective og:image.

Recommendation

Add og:image, og:title, og:description, and og:url meta tags to the homepage. Use the existing hero vehicle lifestyle image (resized to at least 1200×630px per Open Graph specification) as the og:image. The og:title and og:description should mirror the recommended page title and meta description copy. This is a one-time Shopify theme edit to the layout/theme.liquid or snippets/head-tags.liquid file.

Why this matters

Unbranded or missing social thumbnails reduce link-share click-through and present an unpolished appearance when the store URL surfaces in Slack, Discord, social posts, or AI-summarized shopping recommendations — channels that drive a growing share of enthusiast automotive community traffic.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · baton.page_head.og_image
    baton.page_head.og_image is null — no og:image meta tag present in server-rendered HTML.
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    Hero section features a high-quality Subaru WRX STI lifestyle photo that would serve as a strong og:image candidate if the tag were implemented.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
CS-05LOWSearch Findability
Canonical Tag Is Self-Referencing, HTTPS, and Correctly Structured
The desktop canonical tag is implemented correctly — absolute HTTPS URL, lowercase, self-referencing, and matches the homepage URL exactly. This prevents www/non-www variants from competing for link equity.
What changes: No change needed. Verify after any theme update — Shopify theme changes are the most common way canonical implementations break.

Element

`link[rel="canonical"]` (href `https://www.awdmods.com/`)

Observation

The homepage canonical tag reads `https://www.awdmods.com/` — absolute URL with HTTPS scheme, www subdomain, lowercase path, trailing slash consistent with the site's root URL format. This self-referencing canonical correctly signals to Google that the homepage is the preferred URL.

Recommendation

No action required. Monitor canonical output after any theme modification — Shopify custom theme changes are the most common vector for canonical regressions.

Why this matters

A correct canonical at the homepage level prevents any www/non-www or trailing-slash variant from competing with the primary URL for link equity consolidation.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · baton.elements
    Baton elements contain headings at h2 level (e4: "Featured Collection", e31: "Join Our Newsletter!", e32: "Information", e33: "My Account") and h3 level (e5: "Performance", e6: "Exterior", e7: "Interior", e8: "Handling and Brakes") — no element with role "heading-1" or tag "h1" present in the full element index.
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    Hero section shows a vehicle search widget (Make / Model / Year / Trim dropdowns) over a hero background image with no visible H1 headline text. The page opens into a navigation bar and hero image with no textual H1.

Source

Canonical Duplicate Content
SILVEROpen primary source ↗
CS-06LOWSearch Findability
Product Card Image Alt Text Relies on Full Product Titles — Descriptive but Not Optimized

Element

(absent)

Observation

Product card images in the featured collection use proper <img> elements (not CSS background-image), which correctly makes them indexable by Google Images and discoverable via Google Lens. The alt text for the two baton-captured product images (e10 and e11) uses the full product title as the accessible name — e.g., "VelourTex Fitted Carpet Floor Mats for the Ford Focus RS / ST (Set of 4)" — which is descriptive and includes the fitment. This satisfies the minimum requirement. However, the alt text template is the product name verbatim rather than the recommended [Product Name] [Key Attribute] [Angle/View/Context] format. There is no view angle, color, or visual context appended (e.g., "front overhead view, installed in black interior"), which limits Google Lens match quality for visually-intentioned searches. The logo image (e9, AWDMods) carries alt text "AWDMods" which is appropriate for a logo.

Recommendation

Continue using <img> elements for all product images — this is correctly implemented. Enhance the alt text template to append a view angle and visual context after the product title: format as "[Product Title] — [angle/view], [key visual attribute]". For the floor mat example: "VelourTex Fitted Carpet Floor Mats Ford Focus RS ST Set of 4 — overhead view, custom-fit black velour." This is a template change in the product card Liquid snippet that would propagate across all collection-grid cards automatically.

Why this matters

Google Lens processes approximately 20 billion visual searches per month, with 1 in 4 carrying commercial intent — roughly 5 billion shopping-intent visual queries. Automotive accessory products (floor mats, decal kits, carbon fiber accents) are visually distinctive and naturally match visual search behavior. Richer alt text with angle and attribute context improves both Google Images relevance and Lens match confidence.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e10
    e10 alt text (accessible_name): "VelourTex Fitted Carpet Floor Mats for the Ford Focus RS / ST (Set of 4)" — 71 characters, descriptive, includes product name and fitment.
  • DOM · e11
    e11 alt text (accessible_name): "Revo Designs Rocker Stripes Decal Kit for the Ford Focus RS / ST (Set of 2)" — 75 characters, descriptive, includes brand, product type, and fitment.
  • Visual · section-3.jpg @ y=876 (desktop)
    Product card images use <img> elements (tag: img in baton) — correctly structured for Google indexing and Google Lens visual search, not CSS background images.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
ET-01HIGHEthics
Privacy Policy Links to Staging Domain, Not Canonical Store
The footer Privacy Policy link points to a Shopify staging subdomain (`e1520g-k3.myshopify.com`) instead of awdmods.com. Every other footer link uses the right domain — only the Privacy Policy is misrouted. This is a fragile compliance posture: if the staging domain is ever retired or restricted, visitors hit a 404 at the exact moment they're trying to exercise privacy rights, creating an active GDPR Article 13 and CCPA notice violation.
What changes: In Shopify admin, navigate to Online Store > Navigation > footer menu, find the Privacy Policy link, and replace its destination URL with `/policies/privacy-policy` (relative) or `https://www.awdmods.com/policies/privacy-policy` (absolute). Two-minute fix.

Element

`contentinfo` at e34 (footer Information column; href `https://e1520g-k3.myshopify.com/policies/privacy-policy`)

Observation

The footer Privacy Policy link on both desktop and mobile points to `https://e1520g-k3.myshopify.com/policies/privacy-policy` — a `myshopify.com` staging subdomain — instead of a canonical first-party URL on `awdmods.com`. Every other footer link (About Us, Terms and Conditions, CARB Policy, Your Privacy Choices) correctly uses relative paths or the canonical domain. Only the Privacy Policy routes to the staging domain. The pattern is legally adjacent: GDPR Article 13 requires the controller to provide information `at the time personal data are obtained` via a clearly attributable notice; a privacy policy URL under a third-party domain (myshopify.com) rather than the merchant's own domain creates ambiguity about which entity controls the data and which policy governs the transaction. CCPA notice requirements similarly depend on visitors being able to identify the disclosing entity from the URL.

Recommendation

Update the Privacy Policy footer link to point to the canonical first-party URL. On Shopify, the standard path is `/policies/privacy-policy` (relative) or `https://www.awdmods.com/policies/privacy-policy` (absolute). In Shopify admin, navigate to Online Store > Navigation > footer menu, locate the Privacy Policy link, and replace the destination URL. Confirm the `Your Privacy Choices` page also links to the canonical policy rather than the staging domain.

Why this matters

A staging-domain privacy policy URL puts the site in a fragile compliance position. For any visitor from a GDPR-covered country (EU, UK, EEA) or California, the privacy policy is a legally required disclosure. If the staging domain is retired or access-restricted, visitors encounter a broken link at the exact moment they are trying to exercise their privacy rights — producing an active GDPR Art 13 and CCPA notice violation. The fix takes under two minutes in Shopify admin.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e19
    Desktop baton timers field absent. Full DOM scan found no countdown timer elements, 'sale ends', 'limited time', 'only X left', or 'people viewing' strings. Hero and featured collection sections contain no urgency-framing mechanisms.

Source

Research reference
BRONZE
ET-02LOWEthics
No Phantom Social Proof — Reviews Are Truthful With Explicit Count

Element

(absent)

Observation

Review ratings on the featured collection cards display '5.0 / 5.0 (2) 2 total reviews' — a truthful disclosure that names the exact count. No phantom social proof pattern (filled stars with zero reviews, fabricated aggregate badges, undisclosed insider reviews) was detected on either device. No schema-level aggregateRating markup was found in the page_head JSON-LD that would conflict with on-page review counts.

Recommendation

Maintain explicit review count disclosure. As products accumulate more reviews, the star + count pattern will scale correctly without creating regulatory exposure.

Why this matters

FTC Fake Reviews Rule (16 CFR Part 465) prohibits misrepresenting review counts and fabricating social proof. Truthful disclosure of review counts eliminates this exposure class.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e21
    Desktop baton e21 text_content='5.0 / 5.0 (2) 2 total reviews'. Review count is explicitly disclosed as (2). No filled star ratings displayed for products with zero reviews. No 'Rated and Reviewed' aggregate badges without underlying review data. The aggregate rating displayed reflects exactly 2 verified reviews.
  • DOM · e26
    Mobile baton e26 accessible_name='5.0 / 5.0 (2) 2 total reviews' — same truthful disclosure confirmed on mobile.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
ET-03LOWEthics
AI-Generated Images Are Decorative — FTC Materiality Test Passed

Element

(absent)

Observation

Seven AI-generated images (5x Gemini_Generated_Image_* filenames and 2x ChatGPT_Image_* filenames) appear on the page. Their uses are: (1) hero section atmospheric background (car in city scene, alt empty), (2) category tile card backgrounds for five navigation tiles (Performance, Handling & Brakes, Interior, Exterior, Electronics), and (3) newsletter section background (two cars, atmospheric). All actual product listings in the Featured Collection section use real product photography. The AI images do not depict, imply performance specifications, warranty claims, or product capabilities for any specific SKU. Under the FTC Section 5 materiality test and ethics-gate.md Part 5.3 guidance, the materiality threshold is not met: a reasonable consumer would not make a different purchasing decision about a specific product based on the hero banner or category card atmospheric background being AI-generated vs. photographed. No BLOCK is warranted.

Recommendation

No compliance action required. As an optional best practice (not a legal requirement at this time), consider adding a brief site-level disclosure such as 'Some site imagery is AI-generated' in the footer. Major platforms (Amazon, Shopify, Google Shopping) are expected to formalize AI-image disclosure requirements for product listings by 2026-2027 — monitor for updates. Ensure that if AI-generated images are ever used in product-specific contexts (e.g., showing a specific exhaust system installed on a vehicle when no such installation photo exists), disclosure is added at that point.

Why this matters

FTC Operation AI Comply (Sep 2024) targeted deceptive AI capability claims and fake reviews — it did not pursue decorative atmospheric imagery. EU AI Act Article 50 deployer obligations do not reach decorative website backgrounds. The current use pattern is compliant. Continued vigilance is warranted as AI-image disclosure requirements are likely to evolve.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    Hero banner background: ChatGPT_Image_Jan_5_2026_12_36_57_PM.png. Renders as atmospheric backdrop (Subaru WRX in city-night scene). alt='' (empty, treated as decorative). No product specifications, capabilities, or availability claims embedded in the image.
  • Visual · section-2.jpg @ y=513 (desktop)
    Category tiles (Performance, Handling & Brakes, Interior, Exterior, Electronics) each use an AI-generated background image (Gemini and ChatGPT filenames confirmed). Images depict automotive parts/interiors as atmospheric tile backgrounds — not product representations for any specific SKU being sold. Actual featured collection products (floor mats, decal kits, intakes, exhausts) use real product photography.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
ET-04LOWEthics
Free Shipping Threshold Disclosed — No Drip Pricing Violation

Element

(absent)

Observation

The site displays a persistent announcement bar ('FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+') visible above the fold on both desktop and mobile at page load. CA SB-478 explicitly excludes carrier-calculated shipping from mandatory-fee disclosure requirements. The FTC Junk Fees Rule (16 CFR Part 464) is narrowly scoped to live-event ticketing and short-term lodging and does not apply here. No mandatory product-seller-imposed fees (processing fees, service charges, convenience fees) were identified in the homepage DOM. The displayed price format ('From $135.99', '$49.00', '$405.48') is consistent with pre-mandatory-fee pricing. Drip pricing BLOCK conditions are not met.

Recommendation

Maintain the above-fold free shipping threshold disclosure. If any mandatory non-shipping, non-government fees are introduced at checkout (e.g., a processing fee applied to all orders), these must be disclosed on the product page — not revealed for the first time at checkout.

Why this matters

Hidden fees account for 39-48% of cart abandonment. The current transparent disclosure both satisfies legal requirements and reduces abandonment. The FTC Junk Fees Rule and CA SB-478 mandatory-fee BLOCK conditions are clear.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0 (mobile)
    Mobile announcement bar reads 'FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+'. Same threshold disclosure above fold.
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    Announcement bar at top of page reads 'FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+ — Contiguous US only SHOP NOW'. Free shipping threshold is disclosed above the fold on the homepage before any user action.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
ET-05LOWEthics
No Cookie Consent Banner Required — US-Only Targeting Confirmed

Element

(absent)

Observation

The site presents no cookie consent banner on either device capture. This is appropriate for a US-market-only store: (1) hreflang[] is empty — no EU/UK language/region targeting signals; (2) the announcement bar specifies 'Contiguous US only' shipping; (3) CCPA-specific 'Your Privacy Choices' link is present, indicating the operator's compliance posture is US-law-oriented. EU ePrivacy Art 5(3) and GDPR cookie consent requirements apply to sites with EU visitors — absent any EU traffic targeting, no cookie consent banner is required. Note: if the operator begins running EU-targeted advertising or adds EU hreflang tags, a compliant cookie consent banner must be implemented before EU traffic arrives.

Recommendation

Maintain the CCPA 'Your Privacy Choices' opt-out link. If EU or UK traffic is ever targeted (via paid advertising, hreflang, or EU fulfillment), implement a compliant cookie consent banner before enabling EU-targeted campaigns — prior-consent requirements under ePrivacy Art 5(3) apply from the moment EU visitors land on the page.

Why this matters

ePrivacy Art 5(3) and GDPR cookie consent requirements have produced the largest cookie-enforcement fines in EU regulatory history (CNIL fined Google €150M and Facebook €60M for consent-interface violations in 2022). The current US-only posture avoids this exposure. Expansion to EU markets without a compliant consent banner would immediately create BLOCK-class exposure.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e18
    page_head hreflang[] is empty (no EU/UK hreflang tags). Desktop baton overlays_detected[] is empty — no cookie consent banner detected at acquisition. Footer copy includes 'Your Privacy Choices' with CCPA icon and '/pages/data-sharing-opt-out' link — CCPA-specific disclosure confirms US market targeting. Cart drawer shipping calculator defaults to US; announcement bar states 'Contiguous US only'.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
ET-06LOWEthics
No Pre-Checked Boxes or Subscription Dark Patterns Detected

Element

(absent)

Observation

The newsletter signup (desktop e35, mobile e29/e33) requires explicit user action — entering an email address and clicking Subscribe. No pre-checked consent boxes, no confirm-shaming opt-out language, no automatic enrollment into paid subscription detected. No recurring billing subscription product is presented on the homepage. ROSCA conditions (automatic negative-option enrollment before billing info collection) are not present on this page.

Recommendation

Maintain explicit opt-in form for newsletter. If transactional SMS or push notifications are added, ensure CAN-SPAM/TCPA-compliant explicit written consent is collected before first message.

Why this matters

Pre-checked subscription boxes and confirm-shaming language are BLOCK-class violations under FTC Act Section 5 and EU DSA Art 25 (for in-scope platforms). Clean pass here eliminates ROSCA/CAN-SPAM exposure from the newsletter and checkout flow.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e35
    Newsletter section (e35) is a standard opt-in form with email input and subscribe button. No pre-checked checkbox, no confirm-shaming language, no hidden subscription enrollment. The form type is 'customer contact[email]' — standard Shopify newsletter signup with explicit affirmative action required (user must type email and click Subscribe). No ROSCA, CAN-SPAM, or dark-pattern BLOCK conditions are present.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PU-01HIGHPerformance & UX
Hero Car Image Implemented as CSS Background — Not LCP-Optimizable
The big hero photograph is rendered as a CSS background image rather than an HTML `<img>` element. CSS backgrounds can't carry the modern browser hints (fetchpriority, picture/source for AVIF) that make hero images load fast. Hero images are typically the page's Largest Contentful Paint element — Google's ranking signal that the Vodafone A/B test linked directly to sales volume.
What changes: Replace the CSS background with a `<picture>` element using AVIF and WebP sources, fetchpriority=`high`, explicit width/height, and a preload link in the page head. Theme-template change to the hero section.

Element

`(CSS background on hero container; no img tag)` at e19 area

Observation

The hero section displays a full-width car photograph spanning the entire 1920×1080 viewport. This image is implemented as a CSS background rather than an HTML `<img>` element — the header/hero DOM slice contains zero `<img>` tags for the large car photograph, and the page `<head>` contains no `<link rel="preload" as="image">` for it. CSS background images cannot carry `fetchpriority="high"`, cannot be described in a `<picture>` element for AVIF/WebP format negotiation, and are invisible to the browser's preload scanner.

Recommendation

Replace the CSS background with a `<picture>` element carrying `fetchpriority="high"`, explicit width and height attributes, and `<source>` entries offering AVIF and WebP before a JPEG fallback. Add `<link rel="preload" as="image" fetchpriority="high">` for the hero image URL in the `<head>`. This is a Shopify theme change to the hero section template — replace the CSS background-image with a Liquid-rendered `<img>` using Shopify's `image_url` filter.

Why this matters

On most product and collection pages, the hero image is the Largest Contentful Paint element. Vodafone's controlled A/B test found a 31% LCP improvement yielded 8% more online sales. A CSS background hero cannot be preloaded and cannot use modern AVIF format, leaving 30–50% potential file size reduction and an unknown but material LCP delay on the table.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e19
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    Full-width hero banner showing a silver Subaru WRX on a night street scene occupies the entire viewport above fold. The hero image is not rendered as an <img> element — zero <img> tags appear in the hero/header DOM slice for this region, and no <link rel="preload" as="image"> appears in the page <head>. The image is delivered as a CSS background, which cannot carry fetchpriority or benefit from <picture> AVIF/WebP format negotiation.

Source

Media Performance Optimization
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
PU-02LOWPerformance & UX
Navigation Information Architecture — Well-Chunked Category Tiles
The five-tile category grid (Performance, Handling & Brakes, Interior, Exterior, Electronics) is well-structured — five top-level segments, 4–5 sub-items per tile, a single CTA per tile. Five options is within the cognitively comfortable scanning range for first-time visitors.
What changes: No change needed. If the catalog expands beyond what fits in five tiles, consider promoting the top five to a primary row with a collapsible secondary row rather than expanding the single row.

Element

`nav` at e18 (content `Shop All`)

Observation

The five-category tile grid (Performance, Handling & Brakes, Interior, Exterior, Electronics) presents a well-structured progressive disclosure architecture: top-level category labels, 4–5 supporting sub-items per tile, and a single `Shop [Category]` CTA. Five options is within the cognitively comfortable scanning range, each tile is visually distinct with product photography, and the sub-item text lists provide information scent without overwhelming detail.

Recommendation

No change needed on the category grid layout. If the catalog expands, consider whether a sixth or seventh tile would warrant promoting the top 5 to a primary row with a collapsible secondary row.

Why this matters

Progressive disclosure at the category navigation level reduces the number of active decisions a first-time visitor must make before reaching a product — a direct contributor to lower bounce rates from the homepage.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e9
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    AWDMods logo renders in the top-left of the header bar at scroll_y=56. The img tag carries loading="eager" (correct) but no fetchpriority attribute. The page <head> contains two font preloads but zero image preloads.

Source

Cognitive Load Management
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
PU-03MEDIUMPerformance & UX
No AVIF/WebP Format Negotiation — Zero <picture> Elements Sitewide
The mobile category carousel correctly implements swipe affordance — there's a partial-card peek of the next slide at the right edge plus a scrollbar indicator below. Mobile users see immediately that the carousel is swipeable.
What changes: No change needed. If carousel engagement is low in analytics, add higher-contrast dot indicators or numbered pagination as a supplement.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The AWDMods logo is served as a PNG with a srcset of multiple PNG-format variants (no WebP or AVIF alternatives). The DOM contains zero <picture> elements across all sections captured in this engagement. Shopify's CDN supports automatic WebP format negotiation when images are served using the image_url Liquid filter with the format parameter, but this capability is not being leveraged — the logo src URL ends in .png rather than using Shopify's automatic format negotiation. AVIF is now supported by ~94.9% of browsers and reduces file size 50% vs JPEG; WebP ~96.4% support at 25–34% reduction. Both are available at no additional cost on Shopify's CDN.

Recommendation

For the logo and all product images, switch to Shopify's Liquid image_url filter with format: 'webp' (or use the image_tag helper which handles format negotiation automatically). For maximum compression, configure Shopify CDN to serve AVIF via the format: 'avif' parameter where supported. The ideal implementation wraps product images in <picture> elements with <source type="image/avif"> and <source type="image/webp"> before the JPEG/PNG fallback. For a Shopify Dawn/Ignite theme, this is a template-level change to the image rendering partials.

Why this matters

Serving PNG instead of WebP or AVIF for all page images wastes 25–50% of image bandwidth on every page load. For a homepage with a large hero image and product card grid, the difference between JPEG and AVIF can exceed 500KB in total page image weight — directly contributing to slower LCP and lower conversion rates. The Deloitte/Google study found every 0.1s improvement in page load speed yields 8.4% higher retail conversion.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e9
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    Logo image in header is served via Shopify CDN as PNG (awd_big_3cb61c8f...png) with a srcset of PNG variants. No <picture> element wraps any image on the page. The Shopify CDN image URL pattern (?v=... &width=...) does not include format=webp or format=avif parameters in the captured DOM.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PU-04LOWPerformance & UX
Speculation Rules API Absent — No Prerendering on Collection Navigation

Element

(absent)

Observation

The page head and all captured DOM slices contain no <script type="speculationrules"> element. The AWDMods homepage presents five high-confidence next-navigation candidates (the five category CTAs) and a Featured Collection product grid with 9 product links visible. Prerendering these destinations would make category pages and product pages appear to load instantly for the significant portion of visitors who click through from the homepage. The Ray-Ban A/B test found the Speculation Rules API produced +156% desktop PDP conversion and +101% mobile PDP conversion on their collection-to-PDP navigation.

Recommendation

If Chrome's ~65%+ desktop market share is representative of AWDMods' audience, implement Speculation Rules on the homepage targeting the five category collection URLs and the Featured Collection product URLs. Use eagerness: 'moderate' to trigger prerendering on link hover rather than page load, limiting bandwidth cost. Chrome-only caveat applies — Firefox and Safari users see no change, positive or negative.

Why this matters

The Speculation Rules API is the highest single-change conversion upside available for collection-to-PDP navigation — it eliminates perceived navigation latency entirely for Chrome users by rendering the next page in the background before the click. For a homepage that functions primarily as a navigation layer to category and product pages, this is an exceptionally well-matched pattern.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e18
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    The homepage presents five prominent category CTAs (Shop Performance, Shop Handling, Shop Interior, Shop Exterior, Shop Electronics) and product card links in the Featured Collection. No <script type="speculationrules"> is present in the captured page head or DOM slices.

Source

Research reference
SILVER
PP-01MEDIUMAfter Purchase
No Loyalty Program Signal on Repeat-Purchase Platform
The desktop newsletter and footer show no loyalty program signal — no points teaser, no rewards CTA, no entry point in the My Account column. AWDMods sells to a customer base that builds vehicles across multiple purchases over months or years — a textbook fit for loyalty. Without a homepage signal, returning customers have no reminder the program exists between orders.
What changes: If a loyalty program exists, add an `Earn rewards on every build` link to the footer My Account column and a one-line teaser beneath the newsletter headline (`Plus earn points on every part — redeem for store credit`). If no program exists, build one — the customer base and AOV are ideal.

Element

`(absent — no loyalty entry point in newsletter, footer, or My Account column)`

Observation

The homepage newsletter section and footer carry zero loyalty program signal: no rewards enrollment invitation, no points-per-purchase teaser, and no `join rewards` link in the footer navigation. The My Account column lists only Order History, Wish List, and Track My Order. AWDMods sells high-consideration performance parts to a customer base that builds vehicles over multiple purchases across months or years — a textbook fit for loyalty.

Recommendation

If AWDMods has an active loyalty program, add an `Earn rewards on every build` entry point to the footer My Account column alongside Order History. Add a one-line loyalty teaser beneath the newsletter headline: `Plus earn points on every part you add to your build — redeem for store credit.` If no loyalty program exists, the newsletter section and order confirmation page are the two highest-leverage locations to introduce one.

Why this matters

Loyalty program enrollment rates reach 12-25% of new customers when the prompt appears at or near the purchase confirmation moment. For a specialty auto parts store where a single customer may spend thousands across a year of modifications, a program that converts even one additional purchase from 15% of subscribers meaningfully outperforms most paid acquisition channels.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-5.jpg @ y=2073 (desktop)
    Newsletter section and footer contain no loyalty program teaser, no rewards enrollment CTA, and no mention of points or repeat-purchase benefits. The footer My Account column lists Order History, Wish List, and Track My Order -- no rewards or loyalty entry point visible anywhere in the section.

Source

Loyalty Programs
BRONZEOpen primary source ↗
PP-02LOWAfter Purchase
Newsletter Value Proposition Frames Signup as News Delivery, Not Subscriber Benefit
The mobile footer's `My Account` accordion includes `Track My Order` and `Order History` links, giving customers a persistent path to order status from any page. This reduces support tickets and post-purchase anxiety for customers in the shipping window.
What changes: The baseline is good. Surface `Track your order` outside the collapsed accordion as a persistent text link above the footer so it's visible without an extra tap.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The newsletter section (e35, scroll_y 2073) carries the heading 'Join Our Newsletter!' and body copy: 'New parts, new builds, exclusive deals. Be the first to know when something drops for your Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, or STI.' This frames the newsletter as a broadcast channel for store announcements. For a customer who just purchased a performance part and returns to track their order, this copy offers no post-purchase relationship value -- no build guidance content, no owner tips, no reassurance framing, no community membership angle. The value proposition is 'we will send you news' rather than 'you will receive value as a builder and owner.'

Recommendation

Rewrite the newsletter subheadline to lead with subscriber benefit rather than product announcements. Example: 'Get exclusive build guides, early access to new parts, and subscriber-only discounts for your Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, or STI build.' If AWDMods sends how-to content, installation tips, or owner spotlights, name those benefits explicitly. This reframe shifts the signup from passive news consumption to active community membership -- a meaningfully stronger proposition for an enthusiast audience already invested in modifying their vehicle.

Why this matters

The Post-Purchase Hug content framework (buyers-remorse.md F6, Silver -- Kumar et al. 2014, Psychological Science) shows that value-delivering content during the shipping window and beyond reduces cognitive dissonance and builds loyalty. The newsletter is the primary vehicle for delivering this content, but only if subscribers understand they will receive something worth opening. Benefit-led newsletter copy generates lower unsubscribe rates and higher engagement for content-producing brands relative to announcement-only framing.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e35
  • Visual · section-5.jpg @ y=2073 (desktop)
    Newsletter heading reads 'Join Our Newsletter!' with subtext 'New parts, new builds, exclusive deals. Be the first to know when something drops for your Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, or STI.' Purely broadcast framing -- no post-purchase relationship benefit, no content-as-reassurance value, no savings or community membership angle.

Source

Research reference
SILVER
PP-03LOWAfter Purchase
No Referral Program CTA for High-Advocacy Enthusiast Audience

Element

(absent)

Observation

The footer and newsletter section contain no referral program entry point. There is no 'Refer a Friend,' 'Give $X, Get $X,' or equivalent link visible in the footer navigation, newsletter block, or My Account column. Performance car modification is an intensely social, word-of-mouth category: owners share builds on Instagram, YouTube, and at events -- the exact audience most likely to refer peers who own the same vehicles covered by AWDMods (Focus RS, WRX, STI). The 72-hour post-purchase window is the peak referral opportunity, and a customer returning to check order status finds no referral mechanism anywhere on the page.

Recommendation

Add a 'Refer a Friend' link to the footer My Account or Information column that links to a referral program landing page. For the newsletter section, consider adding a one-line prompt beneath the email field: 'Already a customer? Share AWDMods with a fellow RS or WRX owner -- give them $15 off their first order, get $15 back.' The give-first framing (referral-programs.md F4, Silver) outperforms get-first framing and aligns with the community-oriented ethos of the enthusiast audience.

Why this matters

Referred customers from genuine peer recommendations show 16-25% higher profit margin over three years and 37% higher retention than non-referred customers (referral-programs.md F8, Villanueva et al. 2008, Journal of Marketing Research, Gold). For a niche performance parts brand whose customers self-identify through their vehicle (WRX owner, Focus RS owner), peer referrals carry outsized trust. Leaving the brand's most natural acquisition channel -- enthusiast word-of-mouth -- without a structured referral path means relying on organic sharing with no conversion mechanism.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-5.jpg @ y=2073 (desktop)
    Footer Information and My Account columns contain no referral program link, no 'refer a friend' CTA, and no social sharing mechanism tied to purchase or rewards. Social icons (Facebook, Instagram, YouTube) appear in bottom right but are brand-follow links, not referral triggers.

Source

Research reference
BRONZE
PR-01HIGHPrice & Offers
Most Featured Products Lack Price Anchoring
Eight of the nine products in your Featured Collection show bare prices with no compare-at or MSRP — only the Borla exhaust gets the proper strikethrough treatment. Visitors evaluating the $405 intake or $1,766 exhaust have no reference point, so the price reads against whatever they last saw on a competitor site. The single most valuable pricing change available on this page is concentrating MSRP anchors into every card, not just the one.
What changes: For every product whose manufacturer publishes an MSRP, render that MSRP as a struck-through price above the selling price — copy the visual treatment Borla already uses across the rest of the row.

Element

`span.price` at e25 (y=1000, content `From $135.99`)

Observation

Eight of the nine product cards in the Featured Collection display prices as bare numbers with no MSRP strikethrough, no compare-at framing, no `was/now` treatment. The lone exception is the Borla Cat-Back exhaust, which correctly shows `$1,847.99` struck through above `$1,649.99`. For the remaining cards — including the $405.48 Injen cold air intake, the $1,766.00 Magnaflow exhaust, and the `From $135.99` VelourTex floor mats — visitors see a single price with no reference point. Grewal, Monroe & Krishnan (1998) established that an advertised reference price simultaneously elevates the buyer's internal reference price, perceived product quality, and transaction value; without one, the selling price defaults to being evaluated against the shopper's last-seen price, which on this homepage may be a cheaper aftermarket part from a competitor site.

Recommendation

For each product whose manufacturer publishes an MSRP, render that MSRP as a struck-through price above the selling price on the product card. For made-to-order or branded exclusives without a published MSRP, introduce a `Regular price` / `Compare at` value documented from the prior selling price, ensuring it meets the FTC 16 CFR §233.1 bona-fide-price requirement. The Borla card's current execution — red current price with grey strikethrough MSRP — is the correct template; apply it across the full Featured Collection. This is a Liquid edit to the product card snippet that surfaces `compare_at_price` when populated.

Why this matters

Without a reference anchor, high-ticket items like the $405 intake and $1,766 exhaust are evaluated in isolation against the shopper's worst-case mental reference. Real-world A/B tests on reference-price addition report 5–15% conversion lifts on pages that previously displayed no anchor.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e25
  • Visual · section-3.jpg @ y=1000 (desktop)
    Four of the five visible product cards in the first featured row display bare prices with no MSRP strikethrough and no compare-at reference: VelourTex Mats 'From $135.99', Revo Designs Decal '$49.00', Injen Cold Air Intake '$405.48', TufSkinz Gauge Kit '$19.99'. Only the Borla Cat-Back exhaust shows a strikethrough anchor ($1,847.99 struck through, $1,649.99 current).
  • Visual · section-4.jpg @ y=1700 (desktop)
    Second featured row continues the pattern: TufSkinz Mirror Flag 'From $59.99', Magnaflow Exhaust '$1,766.00', VelourTex Trunk Mat 'From $135.99', FoamSkinz Cup Holders '$64.99' — all bare prices with no MSRP or compare-at anchor.

Source

Price Anchoring
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
PR-02HIGHPrice & Offers
No BNPL Markers on High-Ticket Products
On mobile, the $135.99 floor mat is a `Made to Order` item that sits in the exact price range where Buy Now Pay Later produces measurable conversion lift. Without an installment option visible at the card, the upfront commitment friction is higher than necessary. AWDMods can enable Shop Pay Installments natively in Shopify with no extra cost.
What changes: Turn on Shop Pay Installments in Shopify Payments and show `4 interest-free payments of $34.00` beneath the selling price on product cards.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The Featured Collection includes three products priced above $400: an Injen cold air intake at $405.48, a Borla exhaust at $1,649.99, and a Magnaflow exhaust at $1,766.00. None of the product cards display a BNPL installment callout. AWDMods appears to accept multiple payment methods (American Express, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard are listed in the footer payment list at e37/e38), but no installment-payment option is surfaced at the product card level where purchase decisions form. Maesen & Ang (2025) — the first peer-reviewed large-scale field study on BNPL in retail — found that BNPL availability produces a ~9% increase in purchase incidence and a ~10% increase in basket size; the effect is concentrated in the $50–$500 range and above, precisely the price tier occupied by these performance parts.

Recommendation

If AWDMods or any of its brand partners offer Shop Pay Installments, Klarna, or Afterpay on orders through Shopify (the platform powering this store), enable the installment widget on product cards for items priced $100 and above. The Shopify-native format — 'or 4 interest-free payments of $X with Klarna/Afterpay' displayed below the price — is the proven placement. For the $1,649 Borla and $1,766 Magnaflow exhausts, the installment reframe ('4 payments of $412.50') meaningfully reduces the perceived immediate cost and is expected to lift purchase incidence on these high-consideration SKUs.

Why this matters

Performance automotive modifications in the $400–$2,000 range sit squarely in the zone where payment-decoupling has the largest measurable effect on conversion; Maesen & Ang (2025, Journal of Marketing) put the expected basket-size lift at approximately 10%. A customer evaluating a $1,766 exhaust as a lump sum faces a much higher psychological barrier than one seeing '4 payments of $441.50', even though the total cost is identical.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-3.jpg @ y=1000 (desktop)
    The Injen cold air intake at $405.48 and the Borla Cat-Back exhaust at $1,649.99 both appear in this viewport section. Neither product card carries any Buy Now Pay Later messaging (no Klarna, Afterpay, Shop Pay Installments, or Affirm badge or installment callout).
  • Visual · section-4.jpg @ y=1700 (desktop)
    The Magnaflow Competition Series exhaust at $1,766.00 is visible with no BNPL indicator of any kind.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PR-03LOWPrice & Offers
Borla Exhaust Uses Correct MSRP Strikethrough Anchoring
The Borla exhaust card is the one product on the homepage that gets price anchoring right — it shows $1,847.99 struck through above $1,649.99, the textbook two-price treatment. This card is the template for what the rest of the Featured Collection should look like.
What changes: Keep the Borla execution as-is. Apply the same grey-strikethrough-above-styled-selling-price pattern to every other product whose manufacturer publishes an MSRP.

Element

`div.price-box` at e24 (y=1000, height ~60 CSS px)

Observation

The Borla Cat-Back ATAK Exhaust for the Ford Focus RS correctly displays a strikethrough MSRP of $1,847.99 alongside the selling price of $1,649.99, using the visual treatment recommended by Grewal et al. (1998): higher reference price establishes transaction value, lower selling price establishes acquisition value. Savings of $198 are implicit from the two-price display.

Recommendation

This card's price anchoring execution is the template for the rest of the Featured Collection. Extend the same format — grey strikethrough MSRP above, styled selling price below — to all other products whose brands publish a manufacturer's suggested retail price.

Why this matters

Consistent MSRP anchoring across the collection signals price legitimacy and deal quality on every card, not just the promoted exhaust.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-3.jpg @ y=1000 (desktop)
    Product cards across both featured rows show price, review stars, 'Made to Order' badges, and brand names — but no shipping cost indication, no free-shipping threshold callout, and no 'Free shipping' label on any card.
  • Visual · section-4.jpg @ y=1700 (desktop)
    Second featured row continues with no shipping indication at the card level. Footer at the bottom of section-4.jpg shows payment icons but no shipping promise.

Source

Price Anchoring
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
PR-04LOWPrice & Offers
Charm Pricing Applied Inconsistently Across Product Cards

Element

(absent)

Observation

Price endings across the Featured Collection are inconsistent: $135.99, $19.99, $59.99, $64.99, and $1,649.99 use .99 charm pricing; $49.00 and $1,766.00 use round pricing; $405.48 uses an irregular non-round ending that is neither a standard .99/.95 nor a clean round number. Troll & Wieseke's 2024 pre-registered meta-analysis (Journal of Consumer Psychology, 144 effect sizes) confirms .99 endings lift purchase intentions (d = 0.11) without harming quality perception for non-luxury goods — this supports the .99 convention for utilitarian automotive accessories. The $405.48 is the outlier: a mid-digit ending produces no charm benefit (the left digit does not change vs. $405.00) and does not read as a round, premium-positioning price either.

Recommendation

Standardize price endings across the collection. For utilitarian performance parts (intakes, exhausts, floor mats), .99 endings are appropriate and supported by the meta-analytic evidence. Resolve the anomalous $405.48 to either $405.99 (charm, left-digit unchanged — minimal benefit; consider $399.99 instead if pricing flexibility exists) or $405.00 (round). The Wadhwa & Zhang (2015) hedonic-rounding finding is less applicable here — automotive mods are primarily utilitarian purchases — so .99 is the more defensible choice.

Why this matters

The $405.48 irregular ending creates a suboptimal price perception with no upside: it neither benefits from the .99 charm effect (left digit stays at 4) nor from round-number fluency. Standardizing to .99 or a clean round number removes a minor friction point.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-3.jpg @ y=1000 (desktop)
    Price endings visible in section-3.jpg: $135.99 (.99, charm), $49.00 (round), $405.48 (non-standard mid-digit ending — neither .99 nor round), $1,649.99 (.99, charm), $19.99 (.99, charm). The $405.48 is anomalous.
  • Visual · section-4.jpg @ y=1700 (desktop)
    Section-4.jpg prices: $59.99 (.99, charm), $1,766.00 (round), $135.99 (.99, charm), $64.99 (.99, charm). The $1,766.00 round price for the Magnaflow exhaust is consistent but contrasts with the Borla's charm pricing.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PM-01MEDIUMProduct Photos & Video
Product Cards Expose Only One Image — No Multi-Image Access or Count Signal
Every desktop product card shows exactly one image — no thumbnail strip, no `1 of 4` counter, no hover carousel. Baymard requires a minimum of 3 accessible images per listing card. For automotive accessories where fit, finish, and installation context drive the click, single-image cards force every interested visitor to navigate to a full PDP just to see another angle.
What changes: Add a hover carousel revealing 2 additional images (3 total). Sequence: primary packshot → installed/lifestyle shot → detail close-up. Display a persistent `N of N` counter or thumbnail dots.

Element

`img` at e11 (y=989, 271×271 CSS px)

Observation

Every product card across both rows of the Featured Collection presents exactly one image with no mechanism to access additional angles. There are no thumbnail strips, no dot indicators, no `N of N` counter, and no hover carousel. Baymard benchmarks require a minimum of 3 accessible images per listing card.

Recommendation

Add a hover carousel to each product card that reveals at least 2 additional images (3 total accessible) on desktop. Sequence: (1) primary packshot or hero image, (2) installed/lifestyle context shot, (3) detail close-up showing finish or material. Display a persistent `N of N` counter or thumbnail dots above or below the card image.

Why this matters

Shoppers evaluating automotive accessories need to see installation context, fitment evidence, and product detail before committing to a click. When all of that requires navigating to a PDP, a meaningful share of category-page visitors will not make the additional click.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e10
  • Visual · section-3.jpg @ y=989 (desktop)
    Five product cards visible in the first featured-collection row. Each card shows a single static image with no secondary image, no hover overlay, no quick-add button, and no color swatch. No hover state cues are present — the grid is purely static on first render.

Source

Image Quantity Types
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
PM-02MEDIUMProduct Photos & Video
Product Cards Expose Only One Image — No Multi-Image Access or Count Signal
Every category tile background image on mobile has empty `alt=''`. Screen reader users navigating the carousel hear only the heading text — they get no description of what the tile image actually shows. Beyond accessibility, alt text on category cards is also indexed by Google Image Search.
What changes: Add descriptive alt text that names the visual content rather than duplicating the heading. The Performance card showing turbo/intake parts should read `alt='Turbocharger and cold air intake performance parts'`, not `alt='Performance'`.

Element

(absent)

Observation

Across both rows of the Featured Collection (sections 3 and 4), every product card presents exactly one image with no mechanism to access additional angles. The Revo Designs decal card (e11, 271×271px) shows the decal applied to a car's rocker panel — there is no packshot, no additional angle, and no indicator that more images exist at click-through. Baymard benchmarks require a minimum of 3 accessible images per listing card; absence of this access mechanism forces full PDP navigation for even basic product evaluation. For automotive accessories where correct fitment and finish are critical purchase signals, this single-image constraint is a material gap.

Recommendation

Add a hover carousel to each product card that reveals at least 2 additional images (3 total accessible including the primary) on desktop. For cards where photography allows it, the sequence should be: (1) primary packshot or hero image, (2) installed/lifestyle context shot, (3) detail close-up showing finish or material. Display a persistent 'N of N' counter or thumbnail dots above or below the card image to signal that additional images exist even before hover engagement. As a lower-lift intermediate step, an installed-view image visible at hover is significantly better than the current single static image.

Why this matters

Shoppers evaluating automotive accessories need to see installation context, fitment evidence, and product detail before committing to a click. When all of that requires navigating to a PDP, a meaningful share of category-page visitors will not make the additional click — especially for lower-urgency browse sessions. Baymard research finds 50–80% of users overlook truncated gallery images when no count signal is present; the same principle applies to hidden secondary images on listing cards.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e11
  • Visual · section-3.jpg @ y=989 (desktop)
    All product cards in the featured-collection grid display exactly one image each. There are no thumbnail strips beneath or beside the card image, no dot indicators, no 'N of N' counter, and no hover carousel. Users have no visual cue that additional product images exist.
  • Visual · section-4.jpg @ y=1580 (desktop)
    Second row of featured collection cards confirms the pattern: Tufskinz mirror flag accent, Magnaflow exhaust, VelourTex trunk mat, and Foamskinz cup holder insert each show a single card image with no secondary access mechanism.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PM-03MEDIUMProduct Photos & Video
Featured Collection Cards Lack Unified Hit Area

Element

(absent)

Observation

The baton index captures product card images (e10, e11) as standalone `img` elements and brand/title text as separate elements below, with no wrapping anchor element spanning the full card surface visible in the element tree. This is consistent with a split hit-area pattern where the image links to the PDP but the product title and price are either plain text or a separate link — a pattern that Baymard identifies at 76% of sites and characterizes as a consistent source of misclicks and confusion.
