On a 1920×1080 desktop viewport, users moving from the title text toward an adjacent card frequently misclick when the title area is not part of the card's clickable region.

Recommendation

Wrap each product card's full surface — image, brand name, product title, price, badge, and rating — in a single `<a>` element pointing to the PDP. Position any interactive elements that must remain separate (e.g., a wishlist icon or quick-add button) using absolute positioning and a higher z-index above the card link layer, so they remain individually actionable without breaking the unified hit area. Test with keyboard navigation to confirm Tab reaches each card and Enter navigates correctly.

Why this matters

Split hit areas on product cards cause misclicks that interrupt the browse flow and create a perception of a broken or low-quality storefront. For a homepage featured collection that serves as the first product-evaluation touchpoint for many visitors, clickability friction at this stage directly suppresses downstream click-through and add-to-cart rates.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-3.jpg @ y=989 (desktop)
    Baton records e10 and e11 as separate `img` elements at y=989, with product title text rendered below as separate elements. The baton does not surface a wrapping anchor element that spans both image and title, consistent with a non-unified hit area pattern. Card titles render as plain text blocks in the screenshot, visually separated from the image above.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
TC-01HIGHTrust & Credibility
5.0 Stars on 2 Reviews — Skepticism Trigger
The Revo Designs decal kit shows a perfect 5.0 rating based on only 2 reviews. Both signals work against you: the 4.0–4.7 range converts better than 5.0 (perfect scores trigger skepticism), and pages below 5 reviews lose most of the social-proof lift that reviews provide. Aim for 5+ honest reviews; let the rating settle naturally.
What changes: Set up an automated post-purchase email to all buyers of this product asking for a review 7–14 days after delivery. Do not solicit only happy customers — the goal is 5 honest reviews, not a fabricated 5.0.

Element

`div.rating` at e21 (y=466, content `5.0 / 5.0 (2) 2 total reviews`)

Observation

The Revo Designs Rocker Stripes Decal Kit card on the featured collection shows a perfect 5.0 out of 5.0 average based on only 2 reviews. This combination is doubly damaging: the 4.0–4.7 range is the documented conversion sweet spot (Spiegel Research Center, 2017), and perfect 5.0 ratings trigger consumer skepticism about authenticity; at 2 reviews, the page is also below the 5-review threshold where 270% purchase-likelihood lift is realised.

Recommendation

Prioritise post-purchase review collection for this product to move from 2 to at least 5 reviews. Implement an automated post-purchase email triggered by delivery confirmation, sent 7–14 days post-delivery, targeting all buyers of this SKU. Do not artificially inflate the rating by soliciting only satisfied customers — the 4.0–4.7 range converts better than 5.0. Until the 5-review threshold is reached, add a `Be the first to share your experience` prompt to the product page rather than displaying a thin perfect score.

Why this matters

The 0-to-5 review threshold delivers the single highest-leverage trust lift in e-commerce — a 270% purchase likelihood increase. A perfect 5.0 on 2 reviews simultaneously signals inauthenticity, negating the trust benefit of any rating. At an AOV above $49, buyers apply higher scrutiny.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e21
  • DOM · e23
  • Visual · section-3.jpg @ y=466 (desktop)
    Revo Designs Rocker Stripes Decal Kit card displays 5 filled yellow stars with '(2)' review count. Perfect 5.0 rating on only 2 reviews is visible on this featured-collection card.

Source

Social Proof Patterns
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
TC-02MEDIUMTrust & Credibility
No Trust Badges Near Featured Product CTAs
There's no return policy, money-back guarantee, or security signal anywhere near the product cards on desktop. Payment icons live 2,000+ pixels away in the footer. For an independent specialty store selling `Made to Order` parts, visitors need reassurance about returns and security at the moment they're considering a purchase, not after they scroll past the entire page.
What changes: Add 2–3 trust elements to the product card or in a strip below the Featured Collection: a return-policy line (`Free returns within 30 days`), the PayPal mark (highest recognition), and a money-back guarantee phrased positively (`Love it or return it`).

Element

`(absent — proposed location: trust strip below Featured Collection or merged into card template)`

Observation

The homepage Featured Collection carries no trust signals at the product-card level: no return policy snippet, no money-back guarantee badge, no security seal. Payment method icons (AmEx, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard, PayPal, Shop Pay, Visa) appear only in the footer at scroll_y ~2841 — roughly 2,000 pixels below the first featured product cards. Baymard usability research establishes that trust badges function only when placed at the moment of purchase anxiety; footer placement is perceived as generic branding, not as security assurance for the specific product a visitor is considering.

Recommendation

Add a compact trust strip below the Featured Collection section or integrate 2–3 trust signals into the product card template: (1) a return policy indicator such as `Free returns within 30 days` or a link to the returns page; (2) a single recognized payment badge (PayPal achieves the highest notice rate at 67% in CXL eye-tracking research); (3) a money-back guarantee with positive framing (`Love it or return it`) near the Add to Cart area.

Why this matters

Payment icons visible only in the footer provide no trust lift at the point of intent. For an automotive-parts audience evaluating `Made to Order` products at $49–$1,766, the absence of a visible return path and payment reassurance at the product level forces visitors to evaluate risk without the information needed to commit.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-3.jpg @ y=876 (desktop)
    Section-3 shows 10 featured product cards. Only 2 cards display star ratings and review counts (Revo Designs Rocker Stripes with 2 reviews, Tufskinz Carbon Fiber Gauge Cluster with 3 reviews, Tufskinz Mirror Flag Accent with 3 reviews visible in the second row). The majority of cards — including the Lloyd Mats floor mat card, the Injen cold air intake card at $405.48, the Borla cat-back exhaust at $1,649.99, the Magnaflow exhaust at $1,766, the Lloyd Mats trunk mat, and the Foamskinz cup holder inserts — show no star rating widget at all.
  • Visual · section-4.jpg @ y=1580 (desktop)
    Section-4 continuation of featured collection confirms products with no reviews visible: Magnaflow Competition cat-back at $1,766, Velourtex trunk mat, Foamskinz cup holder inserts all lack any rating display.

Source

Trust And Credibility
SILVEROpen primary source ↗
TC-03MEDIUMTrust & Credibility
No Trust Badges Near Featured Product CTAs
The schema markup that tells Google about your store is incomplete: there's no physical address, no phone, no email, no AggregateRating, and the `sameAs` social-link array has 9 empty entries cluttering the data. Quality raters at Google look for these signals when evaluating whether an unknown brand is trustworthy — gaps here cost organic visibility.
What changes: Complete the OnlineStore schema with a physical address (PostalAddress block), phone number, and email. Remove the empty `sameAs` entries — only keep the actual Facebook, Instagram, and YouTube URLs. This is a small edit to the theme's SEO snippet.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The homepage featured collection carries no trust signals at the product-card level: no return policy snippet, no money-back guarantee badge, no security seal. Payment method icons (AmEx, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard, PayPal, Shop Pay, Visa) are present on the page but only in the footer at scroll_y ~2841 — roughly 2,000 pixels below the first featured product cards. Baymard Institute usability research establishes that trust badges function only when placed at the moment of purchase anxiety; footer placement is perceived as generic branding, not as a security assurance for the specific product a visitor is considering.

Recommendation

Add a compact trust strip below the featured-collection section or integrate 2–3 trust signals into the product card template: (1) a return policy indicator such as 'Free returns within 30 days' or a link to the returns page; (2) a single recognized payment badge (PayPal achieves the highest notice rate at 67% in CXL eye-tracking research). If a money-back guarantee exists, positive framing ('Love it or return it') near the Add to Cart area converts better than policy-linked text in the footer.

Why this matters

Payment icons visible only in the footer provide no trust lift at the point of intent. For an automotive-parts audience evaluating 'Made to Order' products at $49–$1,766, the absence of a visible return path and payment reassurance at the product level forces visitors to evaluate risk without the information needed to commit — increasing exit rates and reducing click-through to product pages.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e37
  • Visual · section-3.jpg @ y=876 (desktop)
    Featured collection product cards show product image, brand name, product title, price, and 'Made to Order' badge. No security seal, money-back guarantee, or return policy indicator is present anywhere near the product card CTA zones.
  • Visual · section-5.jpg @ y=2585 (desktop)
    Payment icons (American Express, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard, PayPal, Shop Pay, Visa) appear only in the footer bottom bar at approximately scroll_y 2841, well below every product interaction point on the page.

Source

Research reference
SILVER
TC-04MEDIUMTrust & Credibility
Payment Icons in Footer Only — No Return Policy on Product Cards

Element

(absent)

Observation

The footer 'Information' column lists About Us, Privacy Choices, Privacy Policy, Terms and Conditions, and CARB Policy — but no Return Policy, Shipping Policy, or equivalent customer-assurance link is visible in the footer DOM slice. A return policy link is one of the explicit Trustworthiness elements in Google's Quality Rater Guidelines for product pages, and Signifyd's 2024 European consumer survey found 77% of shoppers base purchase decisions on return policy visibility. The page also carries no return policy or shipping timeline summary anywhere above the fold or at the featured-collection level.

Recommendation

Add a 'Returns & Shipping' or 'Return Policy' link to the footer Information column as an immediate fix. For a higher-impact improvement, surface a brief return policy statement — for example, 'Free returns within 30 days on most orders' — on the featured collection section or in a global trust bar below the navigation. This addresses the most common E-E-A-T Trustworthiness gap for automotive-parts e-commerce where 'Made to Order' items carry return uncertainty.

Why this matters

Visitors evaluating 'Made to Order' performance parts at $49–$1,766 face genuine return uncertainty — custom-made or special-order items often have different return rules. Without a visible return policy, that uncertainty resolves to 'probably non-refundable,' which suppresses purchase intent for first-time visitors to the site.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e34
  • DOM · e37
  • Visual · section-5.jpg @ y=2585 (desktop)
    Footer section shows 'Information' column with About Us, Privacy Choices, Privacy Policy, Terms and Conditions, CARB Policy links. A return policy or shipping policy link is not visible in the footer link list. Payment icons (AmEx, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard, PayPal, Shop Pay, Visa) appear in the footer bottom bar.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
VC-01HIGHHeadlines & Buttons
Hero Section Has No Headline or Value Proposition
On desktop, the entire above-fold viewport shows a hero photograph and vehicle-selector dropdowns but zero text — no headline, no value proposition, nothing that answers `What does AWDMods sell?` for a first-time visitor. The phrase `Car parts, simplified.` only appears as 11px filler inside the selector widget. Visitors arriving cold from search bounce faster when they have no orientation signal.
What changes: Add a clear headline above the vehicle selector that names the vehicles served — `Performance Parts for the Ford Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX & STI`.

Element

`(absent — proposed location: above the vehicle selector dropdowns in the hero section)`

Observation

The hero section displays a full-width dark car photograph with a vehicle year/make/model/trim selector overlay, but contains no headline, no subheadline, and no explicit value proposition copy. The phrase `Car parts, simplified.` appears only as an 11px secondary line inside the vehicle-selector utility widget at the top right — not as a hero headline. A first-time visitor arriving from paid or organic traffic cannot answer `What does AWDMods offer that competitors do not?` within 5 seconds. Baymard and NNGroup research identifies the hero headline as the first of five required above-fold elements for cold-traffic landing pages.

Recommendation

Add a headline layer to the hero containing a specific, differentiated value proposition that names the vehicles served — for example `Performance Parts for the Ford Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX & STI`. Place the headline at a readable size (40px minimum) in the upper-left or center of the hero, above the vehicle selector widget. If the vehicle selector is intended to remain the primary visual anchor, add a one-line benefit statement directly above the selector dropdowns to anchor context.

Why this matters

Visitors without a headline to anchor context bounce faster than visitors who receive an immediate relevance signal. The 5-second-test standard holds that if visitors cannot identify what a site sells before the first scroll, a significant share will leave before engaging the category tiles or featured collection.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    The above-fold area shows a full-width hero image of a white Subaru WRX at night with four vehicle-selector dropdowns overlaid. No headline text appears anywhere in the hero. The only brand statement visible is 'SELECT YOUR VEHICLE / Car parts, simplified.' in the top-right header utility area — not a hero headline.

Source

Hero Section Psychology
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
VC-02MEDIUMHeadlines & Buttons
No Trust Signal Visible Above the Fold
The desktop above-fold viewport contains no trust signal — no review aggregate, no customer count, no media mention, no satisfaction guarantee. For an unknown brand selling $135–$1,766 specialty parts, every first-time visitor must scroll into the Featured Collection before encountering any credibility signal.
What changes: Add one compact trust signal directly in the hero — a review aggregate (`5.0 from verified buyers`) or a customer count (`Parts shipped to 10,000+ AWD enthusiasts`).

Element

`(absent — proposed location: directly beneath the vehicle selector widget)`

Observation

The full above-fold viewport on desktop (1920×1080) contains no trust signal of any kind — no star rating, no review count, no customer count, no recognized media mention, no satisfaction guarantee. The free-shipping announcement bar at the very top (`FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+ — Contiguous US only SHOP NOW`) is the only policy signal present, but it is not a credibility indicator for an unknown brand. The Featured Collection h2 and product cards with review stars appear at scroll_y 906 and below — outside the above-fold zone for a 1080px viewport.

Recommendation

Add one compact trust signal directly in the hero or immediately beneath the vehicle selector widget. Minimum viable: a review aggregate pulled from the existing product reviews (`5.0 from verified buyers`) or a customer count (`Parts shipped to 10,000+ AWD enthusiasts`). Position it at the same vertical level as the vehicle selector dropdowns, left- or center-aligned. If aggregate site-level reviews are unavailable, a media mention or a strong brand qualifier (`Focused exclusively on Focus RS/ST and WRX/STI — no generic parts catalog`) serves the same credibility-anchoring function.

Why this matters

Stanford credibility research (Fogg et al., 2002) found users assess website credibility within seconds, primarily on first-impression cues. An automotive parts site selling $135–$1,766 products to enthusiasts faces a high trust bar; the absence of any above-fold credibility signal means every first-time visitor must scroll into the featured collection before encountering social proof.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e4
  • DOM · e9
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    The full-width top navigation bar renders 'FREE SHIPPING ON MOST ORDERS OVER $75*' at scroll_y 0, spanning the entire 1905px desktop width. The product price of $69.95 is visible in the buy box at scroll_y 403 on the same above-fold view. Both elements are visible simultaneously without any scroll — a visitor sees the free shipping promise and the $69.95 price in the same glance and must reconcile the $5.05 gap.

Source

Hero Section Psychology
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
VC-03LOWHeadlines & Buttons
Newsletter Subscribe Button Is Icon-Only With No Text Label
The newsletter subscribe button on desktop is just a small right-arrow icon with no text. The newsletter copy itself is excellent (it names the specific vehicles), but the unlabeled button creates ambiguity at the commitment point — users have to infer the action from the adjacent input.
What changes: Replace the icon with a text button — `Subscribe`, `Get First Access`, or `Keep Me Posted`. The button is already tall enough; just give it a label.

Element

`button` at e36 (y=2302, height=49 CSS px)

Observation

The newsletter subscribe button at scroll_y 2302 renders as a 46×49px icon-only button containing a right-arrow icon and no text. NNGroup research establishes that generic or absent CTA labels reduce click-through because users scan without context; an arrow icon alone provides no benefit statement and forces the user to infer the action from the adjacent input field. The newsletter section copy itself (`New parts, new builds, exclusive deals. Be the first to know when something drops for your Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, or STI.`) is well-written and audience-specific — the icon button undercuts the surrounding copy.

Recommendation

Replace the arrow-icon-only button with a text CTA: `Subscribe` at minimum, or a benefit-led variant such as `Get First Access` or `Keep Me Posted`. The button is already 49px tall (meets accessibility minimum) but needs to widen to accommodate 2–4 words of label text. Keep the existing newsletter copy.

Why this matters

The newsletter section is the last primary conversion surface before the footer. Icon-only submit buttons introduce ambiguity at the commitment point — a text label costs nothing to add and eliminates the interpretive step users must take when they see a bare arrow.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    The entire above-fold viewport shows the navigation header, hero image, vehicle selector, and category tiles. No review aggregate, customer count, media mention, or satisfaction guarantee is visible anywhere in the first 1080px of page height.

Source

CTA Design And Placement
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
VC-04MEDIUMHeadlines & Buttons
CTA Button Color Indistinct from Page Link Color
The mobile newsletter copy explicitly names the vehicles served — `your Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, or STI`. This is the most audience-specific copy on the page, and the most relevant prompt for the people who will actually subscribe.
What changes: No change needed. Extend this same vehicle-naming pattern up-page to the hero headline so the relevance signal hits visitors at the highest-attention moment.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The Add to Cart button uses rgb(80, 80, 80) as its background color. The page style data confirms this is identical to the link color used throughout the page (link: rgb(80, 80, 80)). On desktop, where the buy box sits in a two-column layout at roughly 773px from the left edge, the button competes visually with the navigation elements in the header strip, which also render at the same gray tone. CXL's meta-analysis of button color tests confirms that the critical variable is contrast differentiation against surrounding elements, not any specific color — when the CTA shares its color with navigation links, users must read labels rather than follow visual hierarchy to identify the primary action.

Recommendation

If the Add to Cart button currently uses the same hex/rgb value as navigation link text (rgb(80, 80, 80)), replace its background with a color that has no other functional use on the page. The SlingMods logo includes a contrasting red — that red, a high-saturation orange, or a saturated medium green would each establish the visual priority the CTA requires. Run an A/B test with a single color change before committing to a permanent brand update. The change is a one-line CSS edit to the button background-color value.

Why this matters

Shoppers scanning a product page process visual hierarchy before labels. When the primary conversion element shares its color with navigational text, the eye assigns equal priority to both, slowing the decision and reducing the probability of an immediate add-to-cart. The Jackson's Art Supplies A/B test demonstrated an 18.4% conversion rate lift from differentiating the Add to Cart button color alone.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    The Add to Cart button in the buy box renders with a dark gray background. The cluster context styles confirm cta_bg: rgb(80, 80, 80) and link: rgb(80, 80, 80) — the CTA background and all navigational link text share the same color value. On a white or near-white page background, the button achieves adequate text legibility but does not visually differentiate the conversion action from the surrounding navigation and text links.

Source

Research reference
SILVER
VC-05LOWHeadlines & Buttons
Newsletter Section Copy Is Specific and Audience-Named
The desktop newsletter copy names the specific vehicles AWDMods serves (`Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, STI`) rather than using generic `stay updated` language. This specificity sets clear subscriber expectations and qualifies subscribers at signup — a Focus RS owner reads the line and immediately knows the value.
What changes: No change needed. Update the copy when new vehicle models are added to the catalog so the audience-naming stays current.

Element

`section` at e35 (y=2073)

Observation

The newsletter section copy names the exact vehicles served (Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, STI) and the specific benefit of subscribing. This specificity — naming the audience by vehicle — is a strong application of the specificity principle: a Focus RS owner reading `something drops for your Focus RS` experiences immediate personal relevance.

Recommendation

Maintain the vehicle-named specificity. When future vehicle models are added to the catalog, update the newsletter copy to include them. Extend the same vehicle-naming pattern up-page to the hero headline.

Why this matters

Audience-specific copy increases opt-in rates by reducing the ambiguity of what subscribers will receive. The current copy sets an appropriate expectation and qualifies the subscriber at the point of sign-up.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-2-mobile.jpg @ y=820 (mobile)
    The row below the Add to Cart button shows only a heart icon and Add to Wishlist text. No shipping assurance, return policy, or satisfaction guarantee appears in the ATC zone. The footer DOM slice confirms free shipping text is buried at scroll_y 6972: '* Free Shipping Applies to Most Contiguous U.S. Orders - Some Items May Not Qualify'.
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    The buy box in section-1.jpg shows the product title, price ($69.95), PayPal Pay Later widget area, and Add to Cart button. No trust-reinforcing text appears in the buy box zone adjacent to the button. The free shipping asterisk disclaimer and return policy are confirmed only in the footer DOM at scroll_y 1385 — over 1000px below the Add to Cart button position.

Source

Headline Copywriting
SILVEROpen primary source ↗
VC-06MEDIUMHeadlines & Buttons
Zero Social Proof Displayed in Above-Fold Buy Box

Element

(absent)

Observation

The buy box displays a star-rating row at scroll_y 353 showing five empty star outlines and the text '(0 reviews)'. On a 1920x1080 desktop the entire buy box — including this zero-review row — is above the fold and visible to every visitor without scrolling. Research from the Stanford Web Credibility Project and Baymard Institute identifies a visible trust signal above the fold as one of five minimum required elements for cold-traffic conversion. A zero-review count does not meet this threshold — it functions as an absence of social proof rather than a positive signal. The hero-section requirements framework specifies that a trust signal with fewer than 50 reviews should not be prominently displayed; displaying a '(0 reviews)' count actively communicates unvalidated status.

Recommendation

If the product has no reviews yet, remove the empty star row from the buy box rather than displaying zero — an absent rating widget is neutral while a '(0 reviews)' display actively communicates lack of validation. As a replacement, surface an alternative trust element in the same position: the EvolutionR brand reputation or a 'Compatible with all Polaris Slingshot model years' fitment guarantee. When the product accumulates 10+ verified reviews, re-enable the rating display at that threshold rather than at launch.

Why this matters

Cold-traffic visitors evaluating a $69.95 aftermarket accessory rely on social proof as a primary quality signal. Displaying zero reviews removes that signal entirely at the exact moment of purchase consideration. Baymard Institute research identifies the first above-fold trust signal as disproportionately weighted in initial credibility assessments — for a product with no reviews, replacing the empty widget with an alternative trust element is the only path to maintaining credibility in that position.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e2
  • DOM · e3
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=353 (desktop)
    The star-rating row renders at scroll_y 353 in the buy box, displaying five empty star outlines (fa-star-o class) followed by '(0 reviews)'. The star icons are visually rendered as empty/unfilled, providing no positive social signal. On desktop the buy box occupies the right column of a two-column layout at 1920x1080 — the zero-review state is immediately visible to every above-fold visitor.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
VC-07LOWHeadlines & Buttons
Newsletter Subscribe Button Is Icon-Only With No Text Label

Element

(absent)

Observation

The newsletter subscribe button at scroll_y 2302 renders as a 46×49px icon-only button containing only a right-arrow icon and no text (baton e36 text_content is empty string). NNGroup research establishes that generic or absent CTA labels reduce click-through because users scan without context; an arrow icon alone provides no benefit statement and forces the user to infer the action from the adjacent input field. The newsletter section headline 'Join Our Newsletter!' and the copy 'New parts, new builds, exclusive deals. Be the first to know when something drops for your Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, or STI.' are well-written and specific — but the submit button undercuts the surrounding copy by providing no action confirmation.

Recommendation

Replace the arrow-icon-only button with a text CTA: 'Subscribe' at minimum, or a benefit-led variant like 'Get First Access' or 'Keep Me Posted.' The button should be at least 48px tall (already meets this at 49px) but wide enough to accommodate 2–4 words of label text. Keep the existing newsletter copy — 'New parts, new builds, exclusive deals' is specific and on-brand — but close the loop with a CTA label that confirms the action.

Why this matters

The newsletter section is the last primary conversion surface before the footer. Icon-only submit buttons introduce ambiguity at the commitment point — the exact moment NNGroup identifies as highest friction. A text label costs nothing to add and eliminates the interpretive step users must take when they see a bare arrow.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e36
  • Visual · section-4.jpg @ y=2073 (desktop)
    The newsletter section shows an email input field followed by a small arrow-icon-only button (approximately 46×49px per baton rect). The button has no visible text label. The CTA is a right-arrow chevron only, with no 'Subscribe,' 'Join,' or 'Get Updates' text.
